request.question: Who is a retired American track and field athlete, who mostly competed in the shot put who returned four years later to the most populous metropolitan area in the world ?

title: Maren Seidler
Jillian Camarena-Williams
Gerhard Windolf
Tokyo
Marianne Werner
Raven Saunders
Dallas Long
Neal Steinhauer
Charlie Fonville
Randy Matson
sentences: Maren Elizabeth Seidler (born June 11, 1951 in Brooklyn, New York) is a retired American track and field athlete.
She dominated the shot put from the mid 1960s through 1980.
She won the event at the USA Outdoor Track and Field Championships eleven times starting in 1967, including nine in a row from 1972 to 1980.
She was the American champion indoors nine times, 1968-9, 1972, 1974-5 and 1977 to 1980.
She won her event at the United States Olympic Trials four straight times 1968-1980, a feat only equalled by only one woman, Madeline Manning, Edwin Moses is the only man to achieve four.
Jackie Joyner Kersee is the only woman who has won more events at the Olympic Trials, split between the long jump and heptathlon.
She competed in the Olympics three times, making the final twice.
Her 1980 selection was quashed by the 1980 Summer Olympics boycott.
Seidler did however receive one of 461 Congressional Gold Medals created especially for the spurned athletes.
Jillian Mary "Jill" Camarena-Williams (born 2 March 1982) is a retired American track and field athlete who competes in the shot put.
She competed at the 2012 and 2008 Beijing Olympics and has represented the United States both indoor and outdoors at World Championship-level.
Gerhard Windolf (born January 13, 1924) is a German Masters track and field athlete.
He was the 1995 Masters World Champion in the decathlon, beating future world record holder Pekka Penttila.
Four years later he returned to get a bronze medal behind Penttila.
As a combined event athlete, he also has produced medal winning results in a range of other events, primarily jumping events.
He won the world championship in the high jump in 1995, 2005, 2009, and 2015, while getting bronze in 1991 and 1999.
He won the long jump in 2009 and 2015, with a bronze in 1999.
He also picked up a silver in the triple jump and shot put in 2009, with bronze medals in 2005 and 2015.
Tokyo (] , ), officially Tokyo Metropolis, is the capital of Japan and one of its 47 prefectures.
The Greater Tokyo Area is the most populous metropolitan area in the world.
It is the seat of the Emperor of Japan and the Japanese government.
Tokyo is in the Kantō region on the southeastern side of the main island Honshu and includes the Izu Islands and Ogasawara Islands.
Formerly known as Edo, it has been the de facto seat of government since 1603 when Shogun Tokugawa Ieyasu made the city his headquarters.
It officially became the capital after Emperor Meiji moved his seat to the city from the old capital of Kyoto in 1868; at that time Edo was renamed Tokyo.
Tokyo Metropolis was formed in 1943 from the merger of the former and the .
Marianne Werner, née Schulze Entrup, later Ader (born 4 January 1924), is a retired West German athlete who competed mainly in the shot put events.
She was born in Dülmen.
Werner competed for West Germany at the 1952 Summer Olympics, held in Helsinki, Finland, in the Women's Shot Put, where she won the silver medal.

Four years later she returned with the United Team of Germany to Melbourne for the 1956 Summer Olympics where she won the bronze medal one place behind Soviet Galina Zybina for the second Olympics and eventual winner, another Soviet Tamara Tyshkevich.
Raven Saunders (born May 15, 1996) is an American track and field athlete who competes in the shot put and discus throw.
She won three NCAA collegiate titles in the shot put for the University of Mississippi.
She was a world junior medalist in 2014 and the Pan American junior champion in 2015.
She holds a personal record of 19.33 m (63 ft floor(((19.33)*3.28084)−(floor((19.33)*3.28084)) *12) in) for the shot put.
Dallas Crutcher Long (born June 13, 1940) is a retired American track and field athlete, who mostly competed in the shot put.
Between 1959 and 1964 he set six official and five unofficial world records.
Long attended the University of Southern California.
He competed at the 1960 Summer Olympics, where he won a bronze medal behind fellow Americans Bill Nieder and Parry O'Brien.
He returned four years later to Tokyo for the 1964 Summer Olympics and won a gold medal.
Domestically he won the AAU title in 1961 and the NCAA title in 1960–62.
Neal Steinhauer (born August 18, 1944) is a retired American track and field athlete.

He won a silver medal in the shot put at the 1967 Pan American Games, behind Randy Matson, and also won two medals at the 1967 Summer Universiade in Tokyo (gold in the shot put and bronze in the discus).
A one-time American national champion (in 1969).
Steinhauer set his personal best (21.01 m) on March 25, 1967 at a meet in Sacramento.
He was on the cover of the February 1969 issue of "Track and Field News."
Charles Edward "Charlie" Fonville (April 27, 1927 – July 13, 1994) was an American track and field athlete who set a world record in the shot put.
In 1945, he had been named the Michigan High School Track & Field Athlete of the Year.
He won the National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA) shot put championship in 1947 and 1948.
Competing for the University of Michigan at the Kansas Relays in April 1948, Fonville broke a 14-year-old world record, throwing the shot a foot further than the record.
James Randel "Randy" Matson (born March 5, 1945) is an American track and field athlete who mostly competed in the shot put.
He won a silver medal at the 1964 and a gold medal at the 1968 Olympics.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

When composing the query, select the minimal anchor token(s) visible in `question` and/or `summary_1` that point to the missing page or fact. You may append a single short attribute keyword (for example country member occupations record season) only when that keyword is needed to disambiguate the evidence type. Do not introduce any other new tokens.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Use these operational rules when choosing a template:
- If summary_1 explicitly lists two or more distinct page anchors as next-hop targets, combine those visible anchor tokens into one compact multi-anchor query and append a short attribute keyword naming the evidence type (for example country member occupations record).
- If summary_1 flags a single missing anchor or a missing_titles list is present, emit a single-anchor metadata-style query that places the missing anchor token first and appends a concise attribute keyword naming the desired fact.
- When summary_1 names multiple candidate entities and the missing fact concerns one candidate’s membership, order, or relation to a canonical collection, prefer a connector-style query that pairs the highest-priority candidate token (first or prioritized in summary_1) with the canonical collection token plus a short attribute keyword requesting membership or order.
- When individual member tokens are present but the needed retrieval is about that individual, prefer member-token + group-anchor + member (or equivalent short attribute) to target the individual’s page rather than issuing a broad group-level query.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Additional constraints (must follow):
- The first sentence must be an answer-first concise identification (a direct answer or concise identification). If the `context` already contains such a sentence, preserve it exactly unless a passage explicitly and directly contradicts it; otherwise derive that first sentence from the clearest passage.
- After the first sentence, append at most one short supporting sentence for each additional relevant named entity only when the passages supply corroborating attributes. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the inputs (context or passages) and paraphrase no more than one or two simple attributes.
- When a queried binary/status attribute for an entity is not stated in the provided passages, indicate that absence concisely within that entity's supporting sentence (for example, with a short parenthetical or clause saying the status is not given in the provided evidence).
- If the passages explicitly assert a relationship between two or more entities that is directly relevant to the question, include one short relationship sentence after the per-entity supporting sentence(s) describing that relationship; do not embed the same relationship claim redundantly in multiple sentences.
- If relevant entities exceed the available supporting-sentence budget under the 1–3 sentence limit, prioritize entities in this order: entities explicitly named in the question, entities named in the preserved first sentence, then other context entities. Do not exceed the 1–3 sentence cap.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Do not infer that the final state is unresolved merely because `summary_1` uses earlier unresolved or bridge language; prefer `summary_2` when it clarifies `summary_1`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

Binary detection is mandatory and must run first. Only if the question is not a binary yes/no or the binary detector does not apply, proceed to the extractive rules below. Use the extractor that matches the question intent and only apply an extractor when the summaries present the required adjacent or verbatim phrasing.

Extraction and formatting rules (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer):
- Person-name selection: if a longer verbatim personal-name string appears in either summary and contains a shorter candidate as a substring, emit the longest verbatim name exactly as it appears in the summaries. Prefer wording from `summary_2` when both summaries provide the same evidence but differ in form.
- Denomination/type extraction: for questions asking a "kind", "type", or "denomination" of a role, extract and emit exactly the minimal token or short phrase that appears immediately adjacent to the role word in the summaries (the word/phrase answering "what kind").
- Event-name extraction: when summaries show a contiguous proper-name followed by a generic event-type noun, emit only the contiguous proper-name core. Strip any leading articles and remove the trailing generic event-type noun; emit only the proper-name portion exactly as given in the summaries.
- Nationality handling: if both summaries unambiguously state the same nationality phrase and at least one summary places the literal single word "citizen" immediately after that nationality phrase, append exactly one space and the single word "citizen" to the nationality phrase. Otherwise emit the nationality phrase exactly as given in the summaries.

General formatting constraints for all outputs:
- Always emit the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries and do not add explanation, context, punctuation, or extra words.
- Do not introduce external facts, additional descriptors, dates, roles, or explanatory text not present in the summaries.
- Do not normalize or invent name variants that are not verbatim in the summaries.
- If the summaries conflict, lack the required adjacent phrasing, or do not present a clear verbatim form for the requested attribute, do not guess—use the module's normal unresolved output.

Output contract:
- Produce a single field `answer` whose contents follow the rules above. The `answer` field must contain only the minimal string supported by `summary_1` and `summary_2` and no additional tokens, punctuation, or explanation.

response.hop1_query: Who is a retired American track and field athlete, who mostly competed in the shot put who returned four years later to the most populous metropolitan area in the world ?
hop1_docs: Dallas Long | Dallas Crutcher Long (born June 13, 1940) is a retired American track and field athlete, who mostly competed in the shot put.  Between 1959 and 1964 he set six official and five unofficial world records.  Long attended the University of Southern California.  He competed at the 1960 Summer Olympics, where he won a bronze medal behind fellow Americans Bill Nieder and Parry O'Brien.  He returned four years later to Tokyo for the 1964 Summer Olympics and won a gold medal.  Domestically he won the AAU title in 1961 and the NCAA title in 1960–62.

Charlie Fonville | Charles Edward "Charlie" Fonville (April 27, 1927 – July 13, 1994) was an American track and field athlete who set a world record in the shot put.  In 1945, he had been named the Michigan High School Track & Field Athlete of the Year.  He won the National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA) shot put championship in 1947 and 1948.  Competing for the University of Michigan at the Kansas Relays in April 1948, Fonville broke a 14-year-old world record, throwing the shot a foot further than the record.

Randy Matson | James Randel "Randy" Matson (born March 5, 1945) is an American track and field athlete who mostly competed in the shot put.  He won a silver medal at the 1964 and a gold medal at the 1968 Olympics.

Jillian Camarena-Williams | Jillian Mary "Jill" Camarena-Williams (born 2 March 1982) is a retired American track and field athlete who competes in the shot put.  She competed at the 2012 and 2008 Beijing Olympics and has represented the United States both indoor and outdoors at World Championship-level.

Marianne Werner | Marianne Werner, née Schulze Entrup, later Ader (born 4 January 1924), is a retired West German athlete who competed mainly in the shot put events.  She was born in Dülmen.  Werner competed for West Germany at the 1952 Summer Olympics, held in Helsinki, Finland, in the Women's Shot Put, where she won the silver medal.  Four years later she returned with the United Team of Germany to Melbourne for the 1956 Summer Olympics where she won the bronze medal one place behind Soviet Galina Zybina for the second Olympics and eventual winner, another Soviet Tamara Tyshkevich.
Carlos Leon (athlete) | Carlos Leon is an American Paralympic track and field athlete who competes in men's shot put.
Scott Winkler (athlete) | Scott Winkler is an American Paralympic track and field athlete who competes in men's shot put.
response.summary_1:
Topic: Dallas Long — retired American shot-putter and 1960/1964 Olympic competitor

- Dallas Long: "Dallas Crutcher Long (born June 13, 1940)" and "is a retired American track and field athlete, who mostly competed in the shot put." (passage 1)
- Olympic facts for Long: "He competed at the 1960 Summer Olympics, where he won a bronze medal" and "He returned four years later to Tokyo for the 1964 Summer Olympics and won a gold medal." (passage 1)
- Other shot-putter mentions (possible distractors): "James Randel 'Randy' Matson (born March 5, 1945) is an American track and field athlete who mostly competed in the shot put" (passage 3) — Matson won "a silver medal at the 1964 and a gold medal at the 1968 Olympics." (passage 3)
- Missing / ambiguous link: the passages do not state that "Tokyo" is the "most populous metropolitan area in the world" (bridge: Tokyo). This external fact is required to map "Tokyo" to that phrase.
- Next-hop retrieval directives (priority):
  1. Fetch the "Dallas Long" article — lede/infobox and "Career" or "Olympics" section to confirm identity and 1960/1964 participation and medals.
  2. Fetch the "Tokyo" or "Tokyo metropolitan area" page — lede/demographics to confirm "most populous metropolitan area in the world."
  3. (Optional) Fetch "1964 Summer Olympics" — section on athletics / men's shot put to corroborate medalists.  
- Note: passages already identify Dallas Long as the athlete who "returned four years later to Tokyo"; if confirming Tokyo's population status is unnecessary for the task, then "no additional retrieval needed."
hop2_docs: List of metropolitan areas in Japan | This is a list of metropolitan areas (都市圏 , toshiken ) in Japan by population as defined by the Statistics Bureau of Japan (SBJ) and the Center for Spatial Information Service of the University of Tokyo.  The region containing most of the people in Japan between Tokyo and Fukuoka is often called the Taiheiyō Belt.

Greater Tokyo Area | The Greater Tokyo Area is the most populous metropolitan area in the world, consisting of the Kantō region of Japan, including the Tokyo Metropolis, as well as the prefecture of Yamanashi.  In Japanese, it is referred to by various terms, one of the most common being National Capital Region (首都圏 , Shuto-ken ) .

List of metropolitan areas in Asia | This is a list of metropolitan areas and urban areas in Asia with the largest population according to several sources.  The Asian continent is one of the fastest-growing continents in the World, with increasing urbanisation and a high growth rate for cities.  Tokyo in Japan is the world's largest metropolitan area by population.  Many other cities, such as Karachi, Kolkata, Chennai, Dhaka, Delhi, Mumbai, Tehran, Seoul, Shanghai, Manila, Osaka, Beijing, Taipei, Hong Kong, Shenzen, Guangzhou, Bangkok and Jakarta are important metropolises.

Keihanshin | Keihanshin (京阪神 , "Kyoto-Osaka-Kobe") is a metropolitan region in Japan encompassing the metropolitan areas of the cities of Kyoto in Kyoto Prefecture, Osaka in Osaka Prefecture and Kobe in Hyōgo Prefecture.  The entire region has a population (as of 2010 ) of 19,341,976 over an area of 13,033 km2 .  It is the second-most-populated urban region in Japan (after the Greater Tokyo area), containing approximately 15% of Japan's population.

Osaka | Osaka (大阪市 , Ōsaka-shi ) (] ;    ) is a designated city in the Kansai region of Japan.  It is the capital city of Osaka Prefecture and the largest component of the Keihanshin Metropolitan Area, the second largest metropolitan area in Japan and among the largest in the world with over 19 million inhabitants.  Situated at the mouth of the Yodo River on Osaka Bay, Osaka is the second largest city in Japan by daytime population after Tokyo's 23 wards and the third largest city by nighttime population after Tokyo's 23 wards and Yokohama, serving as a major economic hub for the country.

Shinjuku | Shinjuku (新宿区 , Shinjuku-ku , "New Lodge") is a special ward in Tokyo, Japan.  It is a major commercial and administrative centre, housing the busiest railway station in the world (Shinjuku Station) and the Tokyo Metropolitan Government Building, the administration centre for the government of Tokyo.  As of 2015, the ward has an estimated population of 337,556 and a population density of 18,517 people per km².  The total area is 18.23 km².

Tokyo | Tokyo (] , ), officially Tokyo Metropolis, is the capital of Japan and one of its 47 prefectures.  The Greater Tokyo Area is the most populous metropolitan area in the world.  It is the seat of the Emperor of Japan and the Japanese government.  Tokyo is in the Kantō region on the southeastern side of the main island Honshu and includes the Izu Islands and Ogasawara Islands.  Formerly known as Edo, it has been the de facto seat of government since 1603 when Shogun Tokugawa Ieyasu made the city his headquarters.  It officially became the capital after Emperor Meiji moved his seat to the city from the old capital of Kyoto in 1868; at that time Edo was renamed Tokyo.  Tokyo Metropolis was formed in 1943 from the merger of the former and the .
